petitioner_background: The petitioner is a rugby team coach (self-petitioning as both petitioner and beneficiary) seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in athletics under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. He was previously a rugby player who transitioned to coaching in 2002, and has been involved in the sport as a player, manager, referee, and coach. His career includes serving as Team Manager and Leader for a delegation in 2003, team coach roles, tour manager roles, and serving as an honorary referee at a 2005 world games event.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: For the one-time achievement, petitioner claimed: (1) his 2004 introduction to and audience with dignitaries on November 4, 2004; and (2) being named Honorary Referee for a 2005 world games event. Supporting documents included a 'Personal Information Sheet,' a September 10, 2009 letter from Dr. [President of organization], a certificate certifying petitioner worked as an honorary referee, and credentials. For lesser prizes/awards under (h)(3)(i), petitioner relied on the same documents, plus evidence of other awards including a Coach Award from September 25, 2004, a Referee Award from September 23, 2005, a Preliminary Award from October 21, 1982, and a certificate dated August 24, 2005. Counsel also cited Wikipedia to support his assertions.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed to show an invitation to meet dignitaries constitutes an award, or that being named an honorary referee constitutes an award. Petitioner provided insufficient evidence about criteria for invitation, how many people were invited, or how honorary referees were nominated/selected. The 'Personal Information Sheet' made no mention of an invitation; Dr.'s 2009 letter mentioned the Team Manager appointment but not invitation requirements. The AAO emphasized that one-time achievement awards must be interpreted very narrowly (e.g., Nobel Prize level) — global in scope and internationally recognized as a top award. For lesser awards under (h)(3)(i), counsel's unsupported assertions about traditional recognition of excellence were insufficient under Matter of Soffici. The Wikipedia citations were rejected as unreliable per Badasa v. Mukasey. The petitioner abandoned challenges to the director's findings on the Coach Award, Referee Award, Preliminary Award, and August 2005 certificate by not specifically raising them on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel contended petitioner met this criterion based on: (1) a trainer license, (2) an Award, (3) a certificate issued to petitioner, (4) a Preliminary Award, and (5) a Level 2 Certificate of Success and Diploma. Counsel also pointed to petitioner's membership in two rugby organizations. Supporting evidence included a June 3, 2012 letter from the Vice President stating that Mr. [VP] 'nominated [the petitioner] repeatedly to partake in training and examination courses,' nominated him 'as one of the selected referees' during a world games event, and that petitioner was chosen as part of a panel in national Referee courses where he evaluated and mentored new referees.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence showed petitioner completed training courses and received awards, but did not establish membership in any association requiring outstanding achievements judged by recognized national or international experts. Without documents on membership requirements, petitioner could not show what the organizations required. The training certificates, licenses, and awards did not indicate petitioner was a member of the issuing organization, and the record lacked membership certificates or letters. The AAO noted that licensure is a criterion for aliens of exceptional ability (a lesser classification under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(k)(3)(iii)(C)), not for extraordinary ability. For the two rugby organizations where membership was shown, the record lacked evidence of their membership requirements. Petitioner abandoned issues relating to other rugby organizations by not raising them on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel pointed to: (1) a May 22, 2010 article published on an internet website dedicated to rugby, described as a 'national and international trade publication,' about a competition 'dominated' by the team coached by petitioner, containing only one photograph (of petitioner); (2) an article and a 2004 untitled article published in a publication counsel described as 'a major national news publication'; and (3) a September 2011 article published on examiner.com, described by counsel as 'a web-based publication serving 234 U.S. markets and 6 Canadian markets, with 18.6 Mio [sic] readers monthly.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the May 2010 article did not mention petitioner by name except as a photography credit, so it was not about petitioner relating to his work; the record also lacked evidence about the publishing website's status as major media. For the other publications, counsel's assertions about readership and major news status were unsupported by any evidence in the record, and under Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez, unsupported assertions of counsel do not constitute evidence. The petitioner failed to provide information on the author of the articles as required by the plain language of the criterion. The examiner.com article was only four sentences, mentioned petitioner's name once, and was not about him; the author's byline indicated he worked for a specific outlet, and the national accessibility of the parent website was not determinative — the issue was readership of the subset of webpages. Petitioner abandoned challenges to other published materials by not raising them on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel contended petitioner met this criterion because he served as an honorary referee at the 2005 world games event. Citing a dictionary definition, counsel asserted a referee is 'a sports official usually having final authority in administering a game.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that neither counsel nor petitioner provided any information relating to petitioner's actual duties at the event as an honorary referee. The record contained no evidence showing what petitioner did at the event. Even accepting counsel's definition that a referee 'usually ha[s] final authority in administering a game' (emphasis noted by AAO on 'usually'), the record lacked evidence establishing that at this event petitioner, as an honorary referee, actually participated as a judge of the work of others in the same or allied field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel pointed to: (1) a December 16, 2004 letter from the National Trainer; (2) a 2009 letter from Dr. [President]; (3) a September 22, 2009 letter from Dr. [Chairman]; (4) a 2011 letter from Mr. [of organization]; and (5) two letters dated December 5, 1989, from petitioner to a Chairman expressing appreciation for accommodations and sponsorship. Evidence showed petitioner's involvement as a player, manager, referee and coach, including roles as Team Manager and Leader, team coach, and tour manager.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found none of the evidence discussed or even mentioned petitioner's contributions to rugby as a whole as either original (first or one of the first to have made the contributions) or of major significance (contributions significantly affecting the sport). The AAO noted counsel made only a conclusory statement that the evidence established the criterion, with no legal or factual basis. Citing Fedin Bros. Co. v. Sava, Avyr Associates v. Meissner, and 1756, Inc. v. U.S. Att'y Gen., the AAO held that merely repeating statutory/regulatory language and relying on conclusory assertions does not satisfy the burden of proof.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted petitioner met this criterion because he served as 'Team Manager and Delegation Leader' for a 2003 delegation and as 'Honorary Referee' at the 2005 world games event.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found petitioner failed to show the delegation event constituted an organization or establishment with a distinguished reputation — insufficient evidence was provided on its reputation. Additionally, petitioner did not show he performed a leading or critical role for the organization as a whole, rather than just for one competition in 2003; his CV indicated no further involvement. For the honorary referee role, petitioner provided insufficient evidence on his actual duties, the total number of honorary referees, how many matches he participated in, or what role honorary referees played. Petitioner also failed to show the world games event had a distinguished reputation. Petitioner abandoned claims regarding other organizations by not specifically maintaining them on appeal.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to satisfy the antecedent regulatory requirement of presenting evidence under at least three of the ten regulatory categories at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i)-(x), nor did he establish a qualifying one-time achievement (major internationally recognized award). Applying Kazarian's two-part approach, the AAO found the petitioner failed at the initial counting step, so no final merits determination was required. The AAO noted that while the evidence was also not indicative of a level of expertise consistent with the small percentage at the very top of the field or sustained national or international acclaim, it did not need to explain that conclusion in a final merits determination. Each basis was considered an independent and alternate basis for the decision, and the petitioner did not meet his burden under section 291 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Lee v. Ziglar, 237 F. Supp. 2d 914 (N.D. Ill. 2002)
Sepulveda v. United States Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Fedin Bros. Co., Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989), aff'd, 905 F.2d 41 (2d Cir. 1990)
Avyr Associates, Inc. v. Meissner, No. 95 Civ. 10729, 1997 WL 188942 (S.D.N.Y. Apr. 18, 1997)
1756, Inc. v. United States Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
Soltane v. United States Dep't of Justice, 381 F.3d 143 (3d Cir. 2004)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO identified procedural issues of abandonment: the petitioner failed to specifically challenge the director's adverse findings on appeal regarding (1) other awards (Coach Award, Referee Award, Preliminary Award, August 2005 certificate) not constituting nationally/internationally recognized prizes, (2) involvement with other rugby organizations for the membership criterion, (3) other published materials, and (4) involvement in other organizations/establishments for the leading role criterion. Under Sepulveda and Hristov, these issues were deemed abandoned. The AAO also applied the Lee v. Ziglar principle that competitive athletics and coaching are not within the same area of expertise, requiring petitioner to demonstrate extraordinary ability specifically as a rugby coach rather than as a player. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
